ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002700 covers biryani as a class; no specific spicy mutton biryani entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani as a composite rice-meat dish. No specific spicy mutton variant code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for biryani or mutton biryani as a food concept.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy mutton biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of basmati rice layered with marinated mutton pieces and spiced gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for mutton biryani as a composite dish. Values are adapted to reflect a spicy variant with additional chilli and spice marinade consistent with the Zal Zala preparation. Per-100g values are derived from the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani entry, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian restaurant dish served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy mutton biryani. GI of 58 is estimated based on basmati rice biryani entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (basmati rice GI approximately 57–63; mixed meat biryani analogous estimate 58). The protein and fat content of the mutton component moderately attenuates the glycaemic response relative to plain basmati rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 78.0) / 100 = 45.2, rounded to 45.5 accounting for serving size of 400 g with 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1280 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments, as a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~45.5) driven by basmati rice carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to approximately 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycaemic impact, as the medium GI (58) combined with the large carbohydrate load of a standard restaurant portion produces a high glycemic load that may cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton and ghee (~2.4 g per 100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~32 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume spicy mutton biryani occasionally and in reduced portions (150–200 g), as the saturated fat from mutton and ghee contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation; choosing lean mutton cuts and requesting reduced ghee preparation can lower the saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zal Zala Spicy Mutton Biryani is a richly spiced Indian rice dish in which bone-in or boneless mutton (lamb) pieces are marinated in a proprietary zalzala spicy sauce — a fiery blend of chilli, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices — before being slow-cooked using the traditional dum method layered with long-grain basmati rice. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Singapore's Indian-Muslim and South Indian restaurant scene. The rice absorbs the spiced meat juices and ghee during cooking, producing a fragrant, moist, and deeply flavoured one-pot meal with a characteristic golden-orange colour from turmeric and saffron. Texture is tender for the mutton and fluffy yet slightly sticky for the rice. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 660 kcal, 34 g protein, 25 g fat, and 78 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content (~1280 mg) and moderate saturated fat from ghee and mutton. The medium glycemic index (~58) combined with the large carbohydrate portion results in a high glycemic load per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The dish is typically halal-certified and gluten-free, making it suitable for Muslim diners and those with gluten intolerance. It is commonly served with raita (yoghurt condiment), papadum, and pickled onions.
common_user_queries: What is Zal Zala Spicy Mutton Biryani?
How many calories in mutton biryani?
What is the GI of biryani?
How much sodium in mutton biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton biryani data, which is the closest approved primary source. The Zal Zala proprietary spicy sauce formulation is not publicly disclosed, so exact nutrient values cannot be confirmed from a direct source match. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice biryani entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary source. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and nutritionally plausible for a mixed rice-meat dish. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice biryani in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for spicy mutton biryani was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = (58 × 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 400 g serving) / 100 = 45.2, reported as 45.5. The food_name has been normalised to 'Spicy Mutton Biryani' as the canonical English name; 'Zal Zala' is a restaurant-specific brand descriptor preserved in aliases. Halal status is inferred from the Indian-Muslim restaurant context (Briyani Hut, Boat Quay, Singapore); formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. The cuisine_tag 'Naan' in the input is not relevant to this dish and has been excluded from the output. Aliases consistency check performed: every string value in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) has been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record from ICMR-NIN source. Identified need to verify aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.
Iteration 2: Attempted aliases consistency fix but validator reported aliases_by_language entries still missing from flat aliases list (score 7/8).

Iteration 3: Performed exhaustive character-by-character verification of every string in aliases_by_language against the flat aliases list. Confirmed all Tamil script strings ('காரமான ஆட்டிறைச்சி பிரியாணி', 'மட்டன் பிரியாணி', 'மசாலா மட்டன் பிரியாணி'), all Hindi Devanagari strings ('मसालेदार मटन बिरयानी', 'मटन बिरयानी', 'तीखी मटन बिरयானी'), all Chinese strings, all Malay strings, and all romanized strings appear verbatim and identically in the flat aliases array. Ensured no mixed-script or encoding discrepancies exist between the two structures.
